LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD sources are downloaded from official upstream locations: a Snap package from snapcraft.io and a terms-of-use page from tradingview.com. No code executes during the `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `--printsrcinfo` commands beyond variable assignments and sourcing the file. There is no `pkgver()` function, and the functions that could perform dangerous operations (prepare, build, package) are skipped by the flags. The global scope contains only variable definitions and assignments, which are safe.
</details>
<summary>Fetching and parsing this PKGBUILD is safe.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a static copy of the TradingView Terms of Service page. It contains standard HTML, CSS, and JavaScript used to render the page. All inline scripts are either data initialization, feature toggles, analytics (GA, Snowplow, etc.), or WebSocket connection logic that connects to official TradingView subdomains (e.g., `static.tradingview.com`, `data.tradingview.com`, `telemetry.tradingview.com`). There are no suspicious commands (curl, wget, eval, base64), obfuscated code, or attempts to exfiltrate local system data. All network destinations are legitimate TradingView services. The file appears to be a normal, unmodified EULA page bundled with the package for reference during installation.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code; standard TradingView terms page.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html. Status: SAFE -- No malicious code; standard TradingView terms page.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for an Electron-based desktop application. It downloads the application from the official Snapcraft API using a specific snap ID and revision, and fetches the terms of use from TradingView's official website. No unexpected network destinations, obfuscated code, or dangerous commands are present. The launcher script reads optional flags from a user configuration file and runs Electron with the packaged app.asar, which is a common pattern for Electron apps. Checksum is skipped for the terms of use HTML because it is a dynamic webpage, which is a common and acceptable practice. No evidence of supply chain attacks, data exfiltration, or backdoors.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR package for TradingView desktop app.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a configuration file for `nvchecker`, a tool used to check for new upstream versions of software. It defines a regex source that scrapes the version string from the official Snapcraft page for TradingView. The URL is legitimate (`snapcraft.io/tradingview`), and the regex is straightforward. The file contains no executable code, no network requests of its own, no obfuscation, and no commands. It is purely descriptive metadata for version tracking. There is no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Simple version checker configuration, no security threat.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/tradingview.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gqg3vtq_...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gqg3vtq_
Analyzing tradingview AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-gqg3vtq_) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source entries: a Snap package downloaded from the official Snapcraft API using a fixed `_snap_id` and `_snap_rev`, and a terms-of-use HTML file from the official TradingView policies page. The `sha256sums` array has a valid checksum for the Snap file and 'SKIP' for the HTML file, which is acceptable since HTML content from a policy page may change and is not code-executable in this context.

The top-level variable assignments and `pkgver()` function (which is absent, so the static `pkgver=3.2.0` is used) involve no command substitution or dynamic execution. There are no network calls or data exfiltration attempts during the sourcing phase. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions contain logic to extract and install the application, but these are **not executed** under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`, so they are out of scope for this gate.

Thus, sourcing this PKGBUILD and downloading the sources via `makepkg --printsrcinfo` and `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` poses no risk of executing malicious code or leaking sensitive data.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous execution during source fetch[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a Snap package from the official Snapcraft API using a fixed revision and known snap ID, which ensures reproducibility and reduces supply-chain risk. The source URLs are legitimate: one points to the Snap binary, the other to the public Terms of Use. The `sha256sums` includes a valid checksum for the Snap file and 'SKIP' for the HTML license file—this is standard practice for non-executable policy documents.

In the `prepare()` function, the script extracts the Terms of Use with `hxextract` and converts it to plain text using `w3m`, both benign operations. The Snap file is then unpacked with `unsquashfs` into a local directory. The `package()` function installs application files (asar bundle, desktop file, icon, license) into appropriate system directories. The generated launcher script reads user-defined flags from a config file and launches the app via the specified Electron version—this is a common and safe pattern in AUR packaging.

No obfuscated code, network operations, or unexpected file manipulations are present. All actions align with standard AUR packaging practices for wrapping a proprietary binary distribution. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or injection of malicious payloads.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Snap-based packaging; no malicious activity detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard Snap-based packaging; no malicious activity detected
LLM auditresponse for tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a terms of service and policy agreement page for TradingView, delivered as an HTML document. It contains standard web content including CSS and JavaScript resources, metadata, and structured data for frontend initialization, consistent with a legitimate website's privacy policy or EULA page.

Analysis shows no evidence of malicious code, unauthorized data exfiltration, or supply chain attacks. The JavaScript included is primarily for analytics setup (e.g., Google Analytics, Facebook Pixel), environment configuration, and session/client detection, all relevant to the operation of the TradingView platform. URLs point to TradingView's own domains (e.g., static.tradingview.com, data.tradingview.com), indicating proper use of first-party services. No suspicious commands like `eval`, `base64`, `curl`, or `wget` are present in a harmful context. The file does not contain any executable package logic, build instructions, or scripts that could impact the Arch Linux system.

This is not a package or script used for installation or build-time processes; it is merely an HTML EULA policy document served via the web. As such, it poses no security threat to the system.

The content is aligned with standard practices for a web-based application’s legal and policy documentation.
</details>
<summary>
Standard EULA page; no malicious content detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed tradingview-3.2.0-71-eula.html. Status: SAFE -- Standard EULA page; no malicious content detected
Reviewed all required files.

Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml...
LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `.nvchecker.toml` is a configuration file for `nvchecker`, a tool used to monitor upstream versions of software packages. It defines a source for the "tradingview" package using a regex pattern to extract version information from the Snapcraft.io webpage for TradingView. 

This configuration only instructs nvchecker to scrape a public web page (https://snapcraft.io/tradingview) to detect version updates. There is no code execution, network request beyond version checking, file manipulation, or system modification in this file. The use of HTTP(S) scraping via regex is standard behavior for nvchecker configs, especially in AUR helper workflows to keep package versions up-to-date.

No malicious or suspicious behavior (e.g., data exfiltration, remote code download, obfuscation) is present. While relying on web scraping can be fragile or subject to spoofing, this does not constitute a supply-chain attack within the context of AUR packaging norms.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for version checking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Configuration file for version checking; no malicious content
